ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this review, the recent advances in the progress of corrosion protective organic coatings containing different kinds of carbon-based nanofillers for the corrosion mitigation of metallic substrates in the chloride solution are summarized. The organic nanocomposite coatings have opened a new field of research for obtaining low-cost coatings with enhanced performance, lifetime, and tailored properties. One of the main advantages of adding carbon nanostructurs to protective organic coatings, is their high barrier performance in corrosion resistance at relatively small amounts of nanofillers. Moreover, these corrosion protective coatings can be utilized in more slender thickness, which lower the expenses. For these reasons, they are considered versatile fillers for polymer matrices. In the following review, the application of the most available carbon-based nanofillers, graphene (G), graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotube (CNT), and carbon black (CB), carbon nanofiber (CNF), and carbon quantum dot (CQD) in the organic coatings for promotion of corrosion protection performance is investigated. The advantages and disadvantages of fillers introduced for anti-corrosion applications are also discussed, and the best filler is selected from the carbon compounds added.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion is considered as a harmful phenomenon, which threatens the durability of infrastructures and leads to immense costs and damage to industrial units and constructions. Therefore, the total cost and environmental consequences of corrosion problems have become a significant challenge for engineers [1]. Corrosion is a continuous process that cannot be entirely prevented in any corrosive environment. Thus, the corrosion prevention strategies are only concentrated on lowering the kinetics and altering its mechanism. These strategies include alloying steel [2], cathodic and anodic protection [3], the use of protective surface coatings [4-6], application of corrosion inhibitors [7,8], or combinations of them [9,10]. Using protective coatings is one of the best approaches in order to prevent the metal surfaces from corrosion.

On the other hand, they also delay the access of corrosive species to the substrate, which leads to the progress of the chemical corrosion reactions on the surface. A set of protective coatings, including organic, hybrid organic/inorganic, conversion, and metallic coatings, have been developed as a barrier to prevent or delay the metal damage by limiting the corrosive materials access to the metallic substrate [11]. The conventional anti-corrosive coatings, which have been almost based on heavy metals (such as chromium, phosphate, zinc, and copper-based compounds), are toxic and environmentally harmful [12-15]. So, there has been an effort to find appropriate non-toxic materials with less negative impacts on the eco-system and high effectiveness in the prevention of the metals from corrosion.

Nowadays, protection of the metal surface against corrosive media by organic coatings is the most conventional and cost-effective method [16]. Organic coatings have considerable physical shielding properties against the penetration of corrosive species, which could significantly delay the reaching time of the corrosive species to the substrate. Furthermore, organic coatings can protect by sacrificial action, or by active corrosion inhibition provided by incorporation of active/passive pigments into the coatings [17]. However, organic coatings are more or less permeable to corrosive species [18]. Free volumes and defects are almost present inside the coating, which could be generated during the coating application or curing processes, leading to the generation of microscopic pathways in the coatings [19]. Therefore, the coatings cannot supply long term corrosion protection. To progress the corrosion protection properties of the organic coatings, various approaches have been proposed. Among the suggested strategies, incorporation of different fillers and anti-corrosive pigments has been detected impressive in the enhancement of corrosion protection. Generally, the anticorrosive pigments can be separated into three categories involving barrier inorganic/organic pigments (e.g., lamellar aluminum pigment [20,21], micaceous iron oxide [22], zinc oxide, and glass flake [23,24], sacrificial metallic pigments (e.g., zinc powder [25]) and inhibitive active pigments (e.g., zinc phosphates [26,27]). In the two past decades, attention has been directed toward the incorporation of nanoparticles into the organic coatings to improve their protection efficiency. It has been shown that nanoparticles such as SiO2 [28], Al [29], Zn [29], Si [30], ZnO [31], Al2O3 [32], TiO2 [33], ZrO2 [34], Clay [35], Fe2O3 [36], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [37], graphene (G) and graphene oxide (GO) [38] provide much better barrier properties rather than conventional microparticles owing to their higher surface area/activity and very lower usage content in the coating matrix. Nanoparticles can effectively fill the microscopic porosities and defects existed within the coating and increase the electrolyte pathway length. Among the available nanofillers, the carbon-based nanofillers (Fig. 1 ) especially CNT and graphene due to their super mechanical strength and high thermal and chemical stability have drawn considerable research interest for exploring the functional nanocomposites with enhanced anticorrosion performance [39]. A review of the literature shows that a number of review articles have covered the covalent/noncovalent functionalization methods of carbon nanostructure. There is a need to present a comprehensive overview on the synthesis, functionalization and application of carbon nanostructure especially on the application of corrosion protection. Herein, an outline of the different types of carbon nanostructure derivatives given with the synthesis methodologies adopted for the preparation of CB, CNT, G and GO. A detailed description provided for the noncovalent and covalent approaches adopted for the synthesis of carbon nanostructure derivatives. The basics of the corrosion protection methods using anti-corrosion coatings and corrosion inhibitors described. The present chapter highlights the application of CB, CNT, G, GO, CNF and CQD its different forms and their functionalized derivatives in corrosion protection namely in the area of anti-corrosion coatings and in corrosion inhibition. In the present review, different methods for the protection of mild steel with oraganic polymer reinforced with CB, CNT, G and GO, the different routes of chemical functionalization of the nanomaterials and their application in anticorrosion coatings and corrosion inhibitors explained. Some drawbacks identified, and the scope of further research in this area has been outlined.

CB is extensively used in the painting industry as a colorant or pigment and can be used as an ultraviolet stabilizer to extend the lifetime of the coatings. It appears that the nanocomposite coatings containing CB particles are very promising as materials for corrosion protection of steel [41]. The high specific surface area and/or activity of CB are involved in the intense interfacial connection of coating to the CB nanoparticles. Incorporation of nanoparticles into the coatings causes increased coating integrity since the particles are finely dispersed and have filled the cavities [42], crack deflection, crack bridging, and crack bowing [43]. Besides, in the primary stage of curing, nanoparticles can be transferred into the coating and fill the defects and reduce the total free volume within the coating. Thus, the packing density of the composite would be improved. As well as, nanoparticles can hinder the coating disaggregation and lead to the improvement of coating homogeneity. These nanocomposite coatings supply remarkable barrier properties against corrosive species [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2-1- Protective performance/mechanisms of CB based coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Wei et al. evaluated the corrosion protection capacity of epoxy/CB nanocomposite on mild steel in saline solution [46]. It was found that the corrosion resistance of the coated substrate was significantly improved above the percolation threshold of CB nanoparticles. The high electric conduction was caused by the conductive CB network in the coating at this CB weight fraction. They showed that the amount of the permeated electrolyte into the coating was slightly changing the dielectric properties of the coating during the immersion period of 400 days. Foyet et al. revealed that increasing CB fraction resulting in a more conductive coating with improved protection performance [47]. However, Zhang et al. believed that higher CB content caused more agglomeration, which decreased the corrosion resistance of the coating [41]. Foyet et al. [47] inspected the barrier property of epoxy coating in the absence and presence of the CB via Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) evaluations. They revealed that both the neat and 1% CB-contained coatings (Fig. 2 a & b) possessed an excellent barrier property, which is stable up to 140 days immersion in the corrosive solution. Above the percolation threshold, corrosive ions transport to the coating/substrate interface and subsequent corrosion reactions progress led to a slight decrement of coating resistance with the immersion time (Fig. 2c). However, the electrical conductivity of the composite dominates the impedance spectra of the immersed sample. The higher resistance ratio of the neat coating compared to the nanocomposite one after the same period of electrolyte immersion was concluded, which was due to the fast delamination of the coating without nano CB (Fig. 2d). As a mechanistic point of view, the CB particles provide conducting pathways into the coating. Therefore the electrical contacts may be created between the metal surface and the coating matrix or between the intermetallic compound on the substrate and the composite structure. On the other hand, the presence of CB at the coating/metal interface impede the localization of generated electrons at the defective coating regions. As a consequence, the formation of interfacial OH− species caused by oxygen reduction at the defective coating sites and so the disbanding of the coating can be limited. Electrons at the defect can liberate into the coating as well as the interface. These electrons spreading into the coating will decrease the rate of oxygen reduction and the metal dissolution at the interface and thereby impede the corrosion process [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2-2-Effect of CB treatment on the coating protection properties
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned before, the CB nanoparticles have a large specific surface area and high activity, which results in a broad agglomeration tendency [49]. The van der Waals forces between the particles and the electrostatic attraction, which is originated from the existed charges on their surface, causing particle agglomeration [50]. The addition of surfactants is a conventional strategy to inhibit the agglomeration of the nanoparticles by adsorbing on the surface of the particles and providing steric hindrance and electrostatic barrier [51]. Various functionalities (such as carboxylic acid and phenolic groups) on the surface of CB nanoparticles can serve as locations for interaction with the primary polar groups of the dispersant.

Ghasemi et al. successfully obtained an appropriate dispersion of CB nanoparticles in an epoxy resin, by using sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) as a surfactant [52,53]. Indeed, SDS could interact with CB, and their hydrophilic tails repulse CB nanoparticles to touch each other, thus prevent them from agglomerating in the matrix (Fig. 3 a & b). The modified nanoparticles can be finely dispersed in the coating and fill defects and microspores. Therefore, the penetration of ions into the coating-metal interface can be lowered, and thus the corrosion resistance of the coating can be increased significantly. Fig. 4 (a-c) shows the long-term anti-corrosive performance of the coatings. They observed that the unmodified nanoparticles could fill the defects, but it was slight in comparison with the modified nanoparticles, which is originated from their larger particle sizes.

Moreover, they evaluated the long-term performance of coatings. They revealed that the epoxy coatings containing the modified and unmodified CB were degraded after 3000 h and 1250 h immersion in corrosive media, respectively. This observation showed the superior corrosion resistance of the modified CB/epoxy in comparison with the unmodified CB/epoxy. Zhang et al. successfully grafted CB nanoparticles with polyvinyl alcohol to obtain particles with a size of about 60 nm [41]. It was observed that the incorporation of 1 wt. % modified CB nanoparticles into the coating causes a reduction in the corrosion rate of steel drastically.

Carbon nanotubes (Fig. 5 ) [54] have been considered as promising candidates for conductive additives because of their large aspect ratio, the large specific surface area, and the proper electrical properties [55,56]. Also, the CNTs provide excellent mechanical properties, in particular enhanced stiffness, fracture toughness, and Young's modulus (on the order of 270 – 950 GPa) besides high thermal stability. The addition of a small number of CNTs into an organic coating can effectively improve its conductivity, thermal, and mechanical properties [57-59].

Different studies have shown the remarkable enhancement in the corrosion protection capability of the coatings containing CNTs, reflecting the electrical current distribution impact on the metal surface protection against any localized attack. High structure CNTs can also fill the flaws and micro-pores to compact the coating layer and thus reduce the crack growth rate in the coating. Therefore, CNTs can induce appropriate barrier protection in the coatings. As well as, CNTs are able to increase the adhesion between coatings and metal substrates [60-62].

Khun et al. studied the corrosion inhibition performance of an epoxy coating filled with different amounts of MWCNT on an aluminum alloy [63] (AA2024-T3), as shown in Fig. 6 . The blister test showed that by adding MWCNT content, the adhesion strength of epoxy composite coatings was significantly increased. They showed that only incorporation of 0.5 wt.% of MWCNT leads to the improvement in the pore resistance (Rpo) of the nanocomposite coating (about ten times increment relative to Rpo content of the neat composite after 45 hrs immersion in 0.5% NaCl solution). Since the well-dispersed MWCNTs in the epoxy reduced the pathways for ions diffusion into the coating, moreover, the value of low-frequency impedance (log |Z|10 mHz), which is an indicator of the total resistance of the protective system was enhanced from 10 KΩ.cm2 (related to the pure epoxy sample) to ca. 40 KΩ.cm2 for 0.5% MWCNT-contained epoxy coating at the above-mentioned submerging conditions. Wear resistance and adhesion strength of the coatings also were improved by the addition of MWCNT. Results demonstrated that the incorporation of MWCNTs to the epoxy matrix leads to a 29% increment of adhesion strength, which clarifies the bonding reinforcement between the substrate and epoxy. Although, the fracture stress decreased two times with the 0.5 wt. % MWCNT content. This confirms that the MWCNTs could not reinforce the mechanical properties due to weak interactions between the CNTs and the epoxy structure, leading to cohesive strength failure.

Wei et al. prepared the polyurethane (PU) nanocomposite coatings containing 5 wt.% MWCNTs using an in situ surface-initiated polymerization method, which provided a high protection efficiency on stainless steel (SS) substrate [64]. The excellent dispersion of MWCNTs in the polymer matrix was found to improve the barrier properties of the coating. They revealed that MWCNTs provided a channel for the conductivity, which caused the cathodic reactions across the metal-electrolyte interface for inhibition correspondingly. They also showed the superior protection of the composite during the long-time exposure in saline solution (Fig. 7 (a-d)). This figure shows the Tafel and EIS curves of bare and PU/MWCNT coated stainless steel at different immersion periods in the 3.0 wt. % NaCl aqueous solution. It can be seen that there is a significant positive shift of corrosion potential (E corr) and a considerable reduction of corrosion current density (Icorr) for the nanocomposite coated SS in comparison with bare SS. Also, the E corr did not change much over long exposure time, indicating the chemical stability of the nanocomposite coatings in a corrosive media over a long immersion period. Corrosion rate (CR) was calculated to be 1.885 and 0.089 mm/year for bare and the nanocomposite coated stainless steel, respectively (Fig. 7(a, b)). Furthermore, EIS results revealed that the nanocomposite coating offers superior corrosion protection to stainless steel as a consequence of higher values of Rp and Rct and the lower values of Cc and Cdl. Significant protection is attributed to the barriers created by the PU polymer matrix reinforced with dispersed MWNTs, which increase the diffusion path of corrosive ions and thus make corrosive ions more difficult to penetrate (Fig. 7(c, d)).

In another research, Kumar et al. [65] studied the impact of dispersion method based on ultrasonic waves and shear force on the anti-corrosive properties of epoxy nanocomposite containing MWCNT applied on a mild steel substrate. They were concluded that uniform cluster-free dispersion of 0.75 wt. % nano MWCNT into the epoxy composite provides decrement of the corrosion rate up to 2.5 × 10−3 mpy along with lowest corrosion current density (4.55 nA) and corrosion protection efficiency increment over 99% (Fig. 8 ). It was declared that the high aspect ratio of superior dispersed MWCNTs in epoxy structure causes high surface area for cathodic half-reaction and decrease the overpotential of oxygen molecule leading to metal protection from corrosion.

Concerning information obtained from an anti-corrosion mechanism of MWCNT-incorporated structures, it can be noted that the high aspect ratio, the large filler-matrix interfacial area and the high tortuosity of the corrosive ions penetration paths due to its good dispersion within the polymer matrix are believed to be responsible for the permeability diminution of the MWCNT–contained coatings [49]

Zachariah et al. examined the anti-corrosion performance of MWCNT-reinforced polymer coating. Compared with the obvious accumulation of MWCNT in samples made with PBz / MWCNT, coatings made with PBz / PBz-MWCNT solution show a homogeneous morphology and good distribution. As a result, the surface compatibility between the bonded PBz resin and MWCNT increases. With the use of polybenzoxazine (PBz) as a matrix polymer (The synthesis method was shown in Fig. 9 (a,b)), the addition of MWCNT in the non-optimal value had a negative effect on the ability to create an anti-corrosion coating due to the accumulation of MWCNT in the matrix. The addition of 3 wt. % of PBz-MWCNT increased the density of cross-links from 90.28 to 99.91%, resulting in a reduction in corrosion rate from 20 μ/year to 0.179 μ/year (The polarization curve was shown in Fig. 9c). The results showed that the use of MWCNT in the matrix is a good way to increase anti-corrosion properties [66].

Owing to the remarkable properties of carbon nanotube, including electrical conductivity, stability, nontoxicity, and doping priority, its presence in the conducting polymer achieved considerable attention in corrosion protection. The electronic interaction of the conductive polymer/CNT composite coatings, in addition to the barrier property, was introduced as the primary protective mechanism for this type of coating [67]. The un-doping process of conductive polymers and metal oxidation is known to act as the cathodic and anodic reactions, respectively. As a result of this process, a passive film can be formed at the metal-polymer interface, causing a positive shift in the corrosion potential. The passive layer protects the metallic surface against corrosion and retains the doping state of the polymer. The conductive polymer needs to re-oxide to act as an electro-active coating. Since the CNT nanoparticles possess high surface area, they show catalytic role in various chemical processes. It has been observed that the catalytic effect of CNT nanoparticles in the polymer re-oxidation by atmospheric or dissolved oxygen accelerates the enhancement of the formation of the oxide layer. Fig. 10 shows a typical potential/time behavior of coated stainless steel by blank poly o-phenylenediamine as a conductive polymer and its nanocomposite based on MWCNT. It can be seen that the blank polymer coating maintained the potential in the positive region for a short time, and rapidly shifted to the negative region after the failure of the passive layer. Whereas the nanocomposite coating maintained the passivity for a longer time in comparison with the blank polymer-coated stainless steel [3].

The blending of PANI/CNTs with other materials like epoxy and preparing a ternary nanocomposite is suggested to be an excellent way to reduce the porosity of primary binary coating and improve the corrosion protection performance [67]. It has been shown that the presence of CNTs improves the conductivity, mechanical, and thermal stability properties of polyaniline (PANI) as well as its efficiency in the improvement of the corrosion protection [68]. In this combination, PANI is electrogenerated on the surface of CNTs and wrapped them. The scanning electron microscopy results for CNT-based PANI nanocomposites prepared by two types of CNTs with different aspect ratios are presented in Fig. 11
[69]. Martina et al. studied the corrosion protection of co-electrodeposited PANI–CNT composite coating by two types of CNTs with different aspect ratios [69]. They showed that the presence of CNTs catalyzed the monomer oxidation, and the catalytic effect of the high aspect ratio CNTs was considerably better. Based on the electrochemical and FT-IR results, they concluded that the presence of CNTs (regardless of type) in PANI coating led to the enhancement of the corrosion protection of stainless steel. In contrast, low aspect ratio CNTs showed better performance to prevent the diffusion of corrosive ions to the substrate. These micrographs clearly show the wrapping of CNTs by electrogenerated PANI at the surface of CNTs. Researchers proposed that CNTs could interact with aniline and bring charge stabilization and protonation of PANI, as well as it can act as condensation nuclei to promote the polymer aggregation. It has been suggested that the charge-transfer process could be facilitated by the interaction of the PANI with the CNTs network, enhancing the effective electron delocalization [69].

Deyab showed that well-dispersed CNTs in the polyaniline matrix created an interwoven fibrous structure that acted as a conductive pathway [70]. It was suggested that two mechanisms shared in the corrosion protection improvement of the nanocomposite coating. First, the porosity of the matrix is reduced in the presence of CNTs, which leads to more isolation of the aluminum surface against the corrosive ions attack. Second, CNTs could improve the adherence of polyaniline coating to the metal surface. They also revealed the effect of CNTs content and showed that the inhibition efficiency of the coating was increased by CNTs concentration increment (Fig. 12 ). The results declared that the inhibition efficiency of PANI coating enhanced from 75.2% to 98.2% with the addition of 0.8 wt.% CNT due to this fact that the incorporation of CNT into PANI structure causes the decrease in oxygen (14 times) and water (9 times) permeability relative to the neat PANI coating [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3-2-Effect of Carbon Nanotube treatments/modifications on protective performance of coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Conventional CNTs possess a strong tendency to aggregation because of their high aspect ratio and weak interfacial interaction with the polymeric matrix. The aggregated CNTs lead to lower the mechanical properties and corrosion protection degree of the coating. Chemical or physical functionalization are usually followed to facilitate the dispersion of CNTs in the polymeric matrix by improving the interfacial interactions (in the form of physical adsorptions and chemical bondings) between the nanoparticles and the polymeric matrix. Physical interactions consist of the adsorption of polymers to the surface of the nanoparticles, and chemical methods involving the chemical bonding of polymer to the CNTs surfaces [71-73]. The role of grafting of organic groups on the surface of CNT has been studied in a large number of reports [74,75]. It was also found that some methods have been carried out as mechanical methods to overcome the aggregation tendency of carbon nanotubes and uniformly distribute the CNTs in the matrix. Ultra-sonic dispersion [76,77] and high-shear mixing [78] are typical examples of mechanical methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3-2-1-The decoration of CNTs by organic compounds
ce:section.ce:para: Through the covalent bonding of the functional groups to the nanotube ends and midsection, nanoparticles can react directly with the polymer chains [79]. Oxidation using strong acids is known as a modification method for introducing oxygen-containing functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups on CNT surfaces. It has been shown that the oxidized CNTs enhance the interfacial interaction of the particles to the various polymer matrixes and thus improve the state of dispersion of nanoparticles as well as crosslink density of the polymer structure [80]. Also, the reactive oxygen-containing functional groups produced by acid treatment can be converted to other functional groups like amine, acryl chloride, ester, and silane groups [81]. The silanization is a conventional method that has been widely used to increase the interfacial adhesion of CNTs to the matrix. Organosilanes are the most usual silane coupling agents that react with hydroxyl functional groups created on the CNT surface via oxidation or reduction processes [82]. The favored covalent functionalization strongly depends on the selection of the suitable organic molecule, which provides both appropriate graftings to the nanotubes and well reactivity for the matrix.

Hammer et al. prepared functionalized MWCNT with carboxyl groups and added them to the siloxane-polymethyl methacrylate-based coatings. The nanocomposite coating showed high protection performance compared to the blank coatings resulting from high barrier properties of the coating due to high covalent bonding of siloxane and MWCNTs functional groups [83]. They also investigated the long-time corrosion prevention performance of coatings and showed that the nanocomposite coating possesses high resistance during long-time exposers. Incorporation of negatively charged dopant, such as carboxyl-functionalized CNTs polymer matrix, changes the ion transport mechanism to cation specific property.

Salam et al. synthesized poly (o-phenylenediamine) (PoPD)–CNT composite with good protection ability, using purified acid-treated CNTs by cyclic voltammetry technique. They observed that by the addition of CNTs, the polymer deposition was increased because of the high surface area of CNT nanoparticles with a large number of reaction sites. Also, the presence of carboxylic and phenolic groups on the CNT surface caused an enhance in the adsorption capacity of organic compounds. Therefore, the polymerization of PoPD and its copolymerization with CNTs were enhanced, and as a consequence, the passivation of the coated stainless steel in acidic solutions was improved. They revealed that the increase in polymer deposition was more remarkable for SWCNTs compared to MWCNTs [83].

Parabakar et al. successfully synthesized poly(ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT) nanospheres on carboxyl-functionalized MWCNTs in the absence (PEDOT-COX/MWCNT) and the presence (PEDOT-NSP/MWCNT) of a surfactant for the corrosion protection of aluminum in LiPF6 in lithium-ion batteries [84]. Fig. 13 shows the corrosion current versus time for bare and coated aluminum samples and demonstrated that PEDOT-NSP/MWCNT reduced the aluminum corrosion rate significantly. The high efficiency was related to the synergistic effects of the cation exchange of PEDOT and the anion-repulsive of the MWCNT surface, leading to the electrostatic repulsion increment. As represented schematically in Fig. 14 , the PF6 anions can slightly transport towards the aluminum surface, resulting in the metal corrosion mitigation. In the absence of surfactant PEDOT, nanospheres are not able to form, and the outer surface of PEDOT-COX/MWNT is mostly composed of PEDOT the MWCNT core cannot directly be exposed to the electrolyte. Thus, MWCNTs in low percent are not able to contribute sufficiently to corrosion mitigation performance. While in the optimum content of MWCNTs, the synergistic effect can be established, leading to an increase in corrosion protection degree [84].

In another study, polyurethane coatings were reinforced using polydopamine-wrapped carbon nanotubes (PDAs@CNTs). The accelerated tests such as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, salt spray, ultraviolet radiation, and immersion showed signs of corrosion resistance and adhesion strength of PU coating. Also, loading CNTs at higher values ​​(2.0% wt. %) increased the coating's roughness and porosity, which weakened the PU corrosion barrier due to the poor dispersion of carbon nanotubes. In contrast, PDA-wrapped CNTs improved CNT distribution in the PU matrix due to the greater compatibility of PDA with PU compared to CNT. Also, the corrosion resistance of PU coating increased after 7 cycles of the aging test to 107 Ω.‏Cm2. Morphological observations also showed a decrease in PU defects. The presence of dopamine in carbon nanotubes prevented the initiation and propagation of micro-defects in PU coatings during long-term aging tests [85].

In another study by Hou et al. [86], Polyurethane polymer was reinforced by two fillers, including CB and CNT functionalized with an amine group(-NH2). With these reinforcements' participation, an interaction was made between the hard segment of the polyurethane chains and the filler particles. Through the fact that the filler particles were completely uniformly dispersed, the glass transition temperature and thermal stability of the nanocomposite increased. Cross-link density and strong interfacial reactions between filler and matrix were confirmed by SEM and TEM micrographs. After adding the filler, the wetting angle was raised, and the composite's anti-corrosion properties improved significantly. The mechanism for increasing corrosion resistance has been linked to increased tortuosity pathways for ion penetration. Increasing the density of cross-links also reduced ion penetration and enhanced the properties of the barrier shown in Fig. 15 . The Tafel behavior showed that the increase in corrosion potential corrosion to more noble values ​​and a significant decrease in current density was observed at 2.5 wt.%.

Meng et al. Studied four types of carbon fillers, including carbon black, a mixture of carbon nanotubes and carbon black, fullerene, and a mix of fullerene and pure carbon nanotubes in resin 22-K BPA-Epoxide Amidoamne. Various parameters, including viscosity, mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties, were investigated. It was found that the most effective material for electrical conductivity and anti-corrosion properties of the nanocomposite coating system was a combination of fullerene and carbon nanotubes, and its effects on water absorption were also evident. In general, the anti-corrosion mechanism of nanocomposite coating is presented in Fig. 16 . In general, a carbon filler is hydrophobic and has a barrier effect with optimal content. At 1% wt.% of this filler, there was the most exceptional water absorption resistance and other corrosive components [87].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3-2-2-The decoration of carbon nanotubes by inorganic compounds
ce:section.ce:para: Decoration or cladding the nanoparticles by inorganic particles has received considerable attention because the clad materials are favorable for dispersion of CNTs, as well as, the high performance of clad materials can be combined with CNTs. The sol-gel method is mainly used to dope the nanoparticles on the molecular scale [88]. Pham et al. [89] prepared nano-Fe3O4/carbon nanotubes by a hydrothermal method so that the Fe3O4 nanoparticles could be adsorbed on the CNTs surface. The 3 wt.% Fe3O4/CNTs hybrid nanoparticles were added to the epoxy coating to improve its protective performance. Unlike the CNTs, which formed agglomerations, the Fe3O4/CNTs showed good dispersion in the epoxy coating that was related to the interfacial interaction of Fe3O4/CNTs with the epoxy matrix in the presence of Fe3O4 decorated on the CNTs surface. Indeed, Fe3O4/CNTs significantly increased the barrier feature and the corrosion protection degrees of the epoxy coating in comparison with the pure epoxy, epoxy/CNTs, and epoxy/Fe3O4 samples (Fig. 17 ). The Fe3O4/CNTs nanoparticles also increased the adhesion strength of the epoxy coating (ca. 71% relative to the pure epoxy) on the steel surface in comparison with CNTs (only 22% enhancement).

He et al. successfully prepared the Fe2O3-MWCNTs hybrid material via the sol-gel method then incorporated them in epoxy. The Fe2O3-MWCNTs composite material combines the excellent properties of Fe2O3 and MWCNTs with the reduced surface energy of carbon nanotubes to improve the state of dispersion of nanoparticles [75]. The results showed that well dispersion of 2 wt. % Fe2O3-MWCNTs into the epoxy resin leads to the improvement in coating mechanical properties as well as corrosion protection capacity after different soaking times (Fig. 18 (a-d)). On the other hand, the results derived from Fig. 18 confirmed that the corrosion resistance properties of composites coating enhanced after MWCNTs were coated with Fe2O3. At the onset of immersion, the rate at which the electrolyte solution passes through the coatings through the epoxy coating pores is much slower than the rate at which corrosion products are produced at the interface between the coating and the metal substrate. However, the corrosion products have not stabilized and the impedance decreases over time, and the presence of corrosion products indicates that the performance of the coatings has been lost.

He et al., in another study, loaded MWCNTs onto the surface of 3-Glycidoxypropyltrimethoxy silane (KH560) treated mica through the sol-gel method. They observed that the MWCNTs could not place on the untreated mica uniformly while increasing KH560 content leads to the uniform loading of nanotubes on the mica surface and improved dispersion MWCNTs through the silanol reaction with mica particles [90]. As a result of good dispersion, the mica-MWCNTs/epoxy showed the largest impedance compared to the mica/epoxy and the pure epoxy. It can be seen in the surface topography of the coatings after the electrochemical characterization that the corrosion state of the blank epoxy coating was severe and caused the coating's failure (Fig. 19 ). The number of holes on the mica/epoxy surface reduced. In contrast, the best surface situation belonged to mica-MWCNTs/epoxy hybrid coating with an impressive protective performance on the metallic substrate. They also found the superior protection of mica-MWCNTs/epoxy during long-term exposure to saline solution.

Graphene-based materials including graphene (G), graphene oxide (GO), and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheets have received considerable interest in the last decades since these nanoparticles possess superior mechanical, thermal, electrical, and barrier/inhibitive properties [92–95]. Graphene-based nanosheets have been shown to be favorable in the construction of corrosion-resistant coatings with more performance and durability compared to traditional ones [18]. This can be related to their high potential to be served as practical barriers against the permeation of corrosive species, which is due to their layered structure, large surface area, high aspect ratio, and thus impermeability against oxygen, water, and aggressive ions [96]. The graphene film grown by chemical vapor deposition on the metal surfaces was demonstrated to has barrier and anticorrosive properties [97]. Regardless of the high corrosion resistance of graphene film, it is still challenging to prepare defect-free large scale films with long-term protection properties [98]. Hence, graphene-based nanocomposites attracted both academic and industrial interests as barrier materials [99].

Neupane et al. reported a novel procedure for preparing an anti-corrosive silane-functionalized graphene oxide layer on magnesium surface using a combination of hydroxylation, silanization, and dip coating at the same time, which is shown schematically in Fig. 22
[100]. During dip coating, nucleophilic substitution occurred between the amine groups of silane and epoxy groups on the GO surface, and a uniform film was achieved on the amino-silylation Magnesium surface. The low-frequency impedance or total corrosion resistance values of bare Mg, Mg-silane, and Mg-silane-GO after 30 min immersion in the commercial saline corrosive electrolyte were reported to be 586, 3830, and 4782 Ω.cm2, respectively. The nanocomposite coating's corrosion resistance was considerably higher than the blank silane coating since the carbon-containing layer reduced the charge transfer towards the substrate.

Yousefi et al. [101] tried to investigate the reduction of moisture permeance for a polyurethane coating in the presence of large size rGO. They found that water evaporation from PU/rGO compound during nanocomposites preparation leads to the self-alignment of rGO sheets without any external forces. The favorable alignment of graphene nanosheets perpendicular to moisture permeation direction led to a significant increment in the diffusion pathways. As a consequence, the moisture permeability of coating reduced remarkably up to 76%.

Li et al. [102] studied the effect of GO, rGO, and titanate functionalized graphene (FG) on the corrosion protection of a waterborne polyurethane coating. It was concluded that the dispersion states of graphene derivatives strongly depended on their chemical reactivity. Fig. 23 (a) shows the values of coating resistance (Rc) obtained from electrochemical characterization versus immersion time. It can be seen clearly that GO and rGO were more capable of protection rather than FG, which is related to their nanoscopic dispersion states and higher aspect ratio. The photographs of the coatings after the salt spray test are displayed in Fig. 23 (b) and show large blisters and accumulated white corrosion products on the surface of blank PU and FG/PU samples. In contrast, just some blisters are detectable on the GO/PU and RGO/PU samples.

In the research work of Jiang et al.[103], Graphene oxide sheets were prepared with different aspect ratios. This synthesis was performed by simple control of chemical exfoliation, and finally, the composite coating GO (0.1 wt.%)‏/epoxy was synthesized and coated on steel. A different aspect ratio of graphene oxide sheets was obtained by controlling the oxidant and oxidation time values and were named GO-a, GO-b, and GO-c with large to small aspect ratio. The current density distribution maps of the samples coated at different immersion time intervals in 3.5 wt. % NaCl solution was shown in Fig. 24 . In pure epoxy-coated steel, after 12 hours (Fig. 24 a), the average anodic current density in the scanned area increased from 1.91 μA.cm2 to 3.10 μA.cm2 due to the diffusion of electrolyte solution into the coating, resulting in the dissolution of the steel. After 24 hours of immersion, the maximum anodic current density, which was 11.54 μA.cm2, was observed, indicating the accelerated destruction of the metal substrate. However, for GO/epoxy composite coatings, a completely different trend was observed for anodic current density (Fig. 24 a-c). The amount of anodic current density of GO/epoxy composite coatings was 1.69 μA.cm2, 2.02 μA.cm2, and 2.48 μA.cm2, respectively. The maximum delay in the dissolution of mild steel substrate was observed in the sample with the highest aspect ratio, which further reduced the penetration path of corrosive electrolytes into the coating. The mechanism of the different aspect ratios of graphene oxide and the reduction of corrosion rate was shown in Fig. 24e. In the absence of graphene oxide, due to cracks and micropores created in the resin curing process, channels were created for the penetration of chloride ions, water, and O2 molecules. However, in the presence of graphene oxide, by blocking the defects and pores created, the penetration of corrosive ions was prevented, and the path of corrosive ions to reach the substrate increased. As a result, the higher the aspect ratio of graphene oxide in the composite coating created, the better the barrier property of the coating in the aggressive environment. The results of this group showed that the high ratio of graphene oxide dimensions created a very effective method to prevent corrosion of polymer coatings.

Yang et al. [104] successfully incorporated the fluorographene into the polyvinyl butyral (PVB) coating to improve the copper substrate's corrosion resistance. They showed that in the presence of fluorographene, the corrosion resistance of PVB coating was increased significantly. In addition to previously-known barrier properties of the well-dispersed graphene-like structure, fluorographene showed properties that are superior to graphene in corrosion protection. In fluorographene, the p-orbitals of Fluorine can react with π-orbitals of carbon atoms, leading to sp3 bonds creation. In this way, the charge densities can be modified so that graphene's conductivity was decreased by introducing conduction scattering centers. Therefore, due to the insulating properties of fluorographene, the occurrence of micro-galvanic corrosion between the nanoparticles and metal surface at coating defects could be restricted.

The composites of graphene with natural conductive polymers such as polyaniline and polypyrrole have been widely used in various applications, including the protection of metal surfaces against aggressive environments. In the past, different conducting polymers such as PANI, polypyrrole (PPy), polythiophene (PTh), and polycarbazole have been utilized since they are synthesized via chemical or electrochemical oxidation methods. Several interaction mechanisms are also possible for the performance of the conducting polymer, which mainly depends on the type of substrate, its chemical composition, physical properties, using specific conducting polymer, amount and type of dopant used, amount of conducting polymer used, and its relative proportion with other active ingredients in the coating [105-107]. Amrollahi et al. Polymerized a layer of PANI on graphene oxide using an in-situ process, which was confirmed by FTIR, XRD, Raman, UV-Visible, and FE-SEM analyzes. The DPHH test demonstrated the ability to modify graphene oxide with the presence of PANI. The corrosion resistance and weathering of GO-PANI were also excellent. This increase in corrosion resistance was attributed to the positive effect of PANI on the dispersion of graphene oxide in the epoxy matrix. The combination of graphene oxide and PANI also increased barrier properties, inhibited radicals, and enhanced UV properties [108].

Yet et al. [109]successfully grafted polyaniline onto graphene surfaces via oxidation polymerization and then prepared PANI/graphene composites for corrosion protection of steel. The obtained nanocomposite represented remarkable barrier properties compared to neat PANI. Jafari et al. [110] evaluated the synthesized polyaniline/graphene (PANI/G) nanocomposite coating by cyclic voltammetry as a corrosion protective coating on the copper surface. They observed that polyaniline uniformly covered the graphene surface so that the corrosion potential of the metal substrate was shifted to the positive region. Consequently, in the presence of graphene nanoparticles, the corrosion rate of the polyaniline coating reduced from 4 × 10−3 to 9 × 10−5 mm/year. They also found that the surface of the PANI/G nanocomposite is almost remained unchanged and without any defects after 120 min soaking in saline solution (Fig. 25 ). While in the same condition, many pits were found on the bare copper surface, which exhibited severe degradation of the metal surface. They also reported similar results for the electrodeposition of polyaniline-graphene nanocomposite coatings on stainless steel surfaces [111]. Jiang et al.[112] prepared well-dispersed polypyrrole/graphene composites (PPy/G) using in situ solution polymerization and then incorporated them into an epoxy resin with a low percentage (0.05-0.15 wt.%) for the corrosion protection of steel substrate. The nanocomposites displayed much higher corrosion resistance than the pure epoxy, with 50–100 times higher protection efficiency. Due to the hybridization process, the spherical PPy particles were changed to the sheet-like PPy@G particles with higher surface area against the permeation direction of corrosion species. Therefore, the hybrid nanoparticle significantly lengthened the corrosion species pathways toward the steel surface.

Chang et al. [109]prepared polyaniline/graphene composites (PAGCs) by synthesizing graphene-like sheets grafted with 4-aminobenzoyl groups (ABF-G) followed by chemical oxidation polymerization of the aniline monomers with graphene-like sheets. They evaluated the ABF-G reinforced coatings' anti-corrosion performance compared with clay reinforced coatings (PACCs). Due to the higher aspect ratio rather than clay nanoparticles, the graphene-like sheets were more capable of creating more tortuous pathways against the corrosive species diffusion into the polymeric matrix (Fig. 26 and Fig. 27
), so that provided higher efficiency in corrosion protection of the coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 4-2-Effect of graphene-based nanosheet treatments on protective performance
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the high surface area, van der Waals interactions, and π-π stacking, graphene and its derivatives tend to agglomerate or restack, resulting in an inverse effect on their dispersion and distribution in polymeric matrixes [113]. Indeed, without improving the dispersion and exfoliation of the graphene-based nanosheets, it is impossible to apply them in the composite coatings. Schriver et al. [98]even believed that untreated graphene might lead to poor dispersion and thus increase metals' corrosion rate in long-time exposure to the aggressive environment. It is well-known that, like all nanoparticles, which are used as anticorrosive fillers in the coatings, the protection mechanism of conventional graphene-based nanosheets is mainly based on the barrier action these compounds and the lack of active inhibition properties. In recent years, various modification methods, including organic modification and cladding of nanoparticles and in situ polymerization, are suggested for fulfilling the goal [114]. Herein, we have tried to mention the most popular methods for the functionalization of graphene, which are commonly used to prepare graphene-modified coatings.

The chemical modification of G and GO nanosheets markedly reduces their tendency to interact with each other and prevents their stacking and agglomeration. Likewise, it causes improvement of the interfacial interaction of G and GO with polymer matrix via covalent or non-covalent binding [115]. Meanwhile, it provides a simple method to fabricate multi-functional nanocomposite coatings with effectively enhanced protection properties.

Ahmadi et al. [116] successfully functionalized the GO with aminopropyl triethoxy silane (APTES) through the sol-gel method. They added them to a hybrid silane coating composed of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and APTES. The coatings' corrosion performance was investigated on the steel substrate, and results showed the remarkable protective performance for silane coating containing silanized GO compared to the blank silane coating. They proved that the incorporation of silane functionalized GO into the silane film developed a better cross-linking of silanol groups of the film and improved its anti-corrosion properties. Also, the extended diffusion length in GO nanosheets was introduced as another reason for the high efficiency of the coating in corrosion protection. Mo et al. studied APTES-functionalized graphene and graphene oxide's effect on the tribological and anti-corrosion properties of a polyurethane coating on white cast iron [117]. They demonstrated that the functionalized graphene (FG) and functionalized graphene oxide (FGO) could supply both lubrication and PU coatings' barrier properties. Fig. 28 describes the FG and FGO particles' role in the PU matrix, which act in the tribological and corrosion process. It can be seen that a large number of formed debris during friction, were reduced significantly as a consequence of the presence of FG and FGO in the PU matrix, as well as the straight permeation pathways of the corrosive species was changed to tortuous one. They also noticed that the presence of FGO in the coating represented better tribological properties but worse anti-corrosion properties than FG/PU coatings. The numerous oxygenated groups of GO caused more effective modification and, consequently, better dispersion. On the other hand, these groups are apparent reasons for the destroyed structure of graphene lattice responsible for poor barrier properties.

Ramezanzadeh et al. prepared silane/APTES-functionalized GO nanocomposite as a primer to improve the interfacial interaction of the epoxy coating with steel substrate and the corrosion resistance of the coating [118]. The obtained results of EIS, cathodic disbondment (CD), and Pull-off adhesion (before and after salt spray exposure) examinations are displayed in Fig. 29 (a-c), respectively. It can be concluded that surface pre-treatment of steel by SC-fGO can provide proper enhancement of the corrosion protection properties because of the lower delamination of the epoxy coating and less creation of the corrosion products at the interface as a result of the presence of SC-fGO. Very high Rt and |Z|0.01Hz values were acquired for the epoxy applied to the sample treated by SC-fGO. Furthermore, the cathodic disbondment and adhesion results clarified that silane treatment of steel substrates leads to the decrease of the cathodic disbanded area at various saline electrolyte immersion and coating adhesion reinforcement before and after the corrosive medium exposing. It was also elucidated that as a consequence of silane modification, the amine groups were generated on the surface of GO, which resulted in the increased interlayer distance of nanosheets. The well-dispersed FGO decreased the permeation of corrosive ions to the silane layer and presented an amine rich surface with a remarkable tendency to form chemical bonding with epoxy resin resulting in its strong adhesion to the steel surface. Therefore, due to the silane/FGO film's blocking effect, the cathodic disbondment, corrosion products, and adhesion loss were decreased. Moreover, another mechanism responsible for reducing the cathodic disbondment of the epoxy coating was presented in Fig. 29 d1-d2. Basically, due to GO nanosheets' negative zeta potential at pHs>7, the silane/FGO layer would be negatively charged in contact with the electrolyte environments. Hence, the solution's hydrated cations could be adsorbed on the silane nanocomposite layer, preventing the cations from diffusing into the delamination front.

In one study, asaldoust et al. [119]. Incorporated benzoamidazole-zinc phosphate (ZP-BIM) corrosion inhibitor nanoparticles into graphene oxide (GO) and used them as fillers in epoxy resins. After coating on steel, they studied its anti-corrosion application in marine environments. The results of FE-SEM/EDS analysis showed that when the metal-containing coating was placed in the saline solution, a zinc-containing inhibitory layer and compounds containing BIM were formed on the surface. The Salt spray and EIS test result showed that the best barrier performance was achieved in coatings with 0.15% wt.% of GO-ZP-BIM film (Fig. 30 (a-e)). The effect of the combined barrier in the presence of GO sheets and the inhibitory activity of ZP-BIM led to the creation of superior anti-corrosion properties for an epoxy film.

Chang et al. prepared epoxy/carboxyl-graphene composites on cold-rolled steel with hydrophobic Xanthosoma sagittifoliumleaf-like surface by an innovative processing method [122]. The nanocomposite coating's hydrophobicity and barrier feature originated from the low-contact angle of the surface and well-dispersed graphene nanosheets in the matrix (Fig. 33 ), resulted in excellent corrosion protection compared to the usual hydrophobic epoxy and epoxy coatings. Mo et al. also fabricated a textured nanocomposite coating consisting of PU and siloxane-functionalized graphene (FG) using a replication method [123]. Similarly, the developed hydrophobicity by surface texturing and the gas barrier effect of well-dispersed graphene nanosheets in the PU matrix led to synergistic protection of the coating against corrosion.

Motamedi et al. [124] evaluated the anticorrosion performance of epoxy nanocomposite containing 0.15 wt.% green reduced GO nanosheets (Fig. 34 ). They synthesized the rGO nanoparticles using bioactive histamine as a green reductant and zinc cations (RGO-Hist-Zn) by two approaches (Fig. 29a). The barrier-inhibitive activities of RGO-Hist-Zn nanosheets were inspected via corrosion and adhesion tests. The results indicated that the best anti-corrosion performance (log |Z|10 mHz=9.73 Ω cm2 after 60 days’ immersion in NaCl solution along with very low coating detachment and rusts/blisters beneath the coating), self-healing property, adhesion to a steel surface (only 14.2 % adhesion loss after 38 days of immersion) and cathodic disbondment resistance (4.7 mm) were achieved by RGO-Hist-Zn Ι - incorporated epoxy coating compared to the GO-contained epoxy. In the case of the protection mechanism study (Fig. 34b), easy access of the corrosive electrolyte to the steel surface from the coating defects led to the fast initiation of the corrosion reactions at active anodic sites of the steel. The iron cations produce on the anodic sites located at the surface/interface (Fe → Fe2+ + 2e−). Simultaneously, the hydroxyl ions generate at cathodic sites under the cathodic reaction: O2 + 2H2O + 4e− → 4OH−. These reactions accelerate the deterioration of the coating adhesion bonds, resulting in detachment from the coating from the steel and steel severe corrosion. However, the mechanism of steel corrosion prevention improvement by the RGO based epoxy nanocomposite may depend on the suitable dispersion of RGO nanosheets in the epoxy matrix. This event increases the pathways' tortuosity for the diffusion of the O2, H2O, and other corrosive species. It was found from the results that the histamine-zinc-based green reduction of GO and its incorporation into the epoxy coating caused restriction of the corrosion reactions at the epoxy/steel interface. It was found from the electrochemical results that the RGO-Hist-Zn nanosheets behaved as a barrier against corrosive ions transportation through the coating porosities toward the metal surface. In the scratch region, the histamine molecules and Zn+2 cations may liberate from the nanoparticles within the bulk of coating, which creates an inhibitive/passive layer on the substrate having physically-chemically surface interactions (Fig. 34a). Hence, the corrosion protection of the EP/RGO-Hist-Zn nanocomposite on the steel surface may be supported by the barrier-inhibitive dual-functional effect. In other words, the anti-corrosion performance of the EP/RGO-Hist-Zn is not only ascribed to the barrier activity of the well-dispersed nanosheets in the network but also corroborated with the active inhibition functionality of the histamine molecules (with an anodic action) and zinc cations (as a cathodic inhibitor) released from the nanoparticles to the defect region and subsequent adsorption on both anodic and cathodic sites (Fig. 34(c,d)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: 4-2-2-The decoration of Graphene Oxide sheets with nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para:
The decoration of GO nanosheets with inorganic nanoparticles (NP), besides the advantages of both nanofillers, is an excellent way to reduce the GO sheet's tendency to aggregation and develop GO thin nanosheet structure by increasing their interlayer distance. The well-dispersion of thoroughly exfoliated GO-NP hybrids with sheet structure can block the micro-pores successfully, leading to significant improvement in the corrosion protection of the coatings at a low content of nanofiller [125]. Nanoparticles like silica, aluminum oxide, zinc oxide, titanium oxide, iron oxide, and calcium carbonate are widely applied to decorate GO nanosheets [126]. The anticorrosive mechanism of a typical Fe3O4–GO hybrids reinforced nanocomposite coatings is displayed in Fig. 35
[127]. In comparison with GO sheets and Fe3O4 nanoparticles, it is evident that Fe3O4–GO hybrid exhibited homogenous dispersion without any accumulation within the coating, indicating the coating's high protection capability in the presence of Fe3O4–GO hybrid. Individually GO sheets, and Fe3O4 nanoparticles are also able to block the micropores to some extent and provide better corrosion protection than the blank matrix.

Di et al. [127] successfully decorated Fe3O4 nanoparticles on the graphene oxide (Fe3O4–GO) using a silane coupling agent and incorporated them into an epoxy coating (EP). They showed that Fe3O4–GO hybrid nanoparticles in the epoxy coating lead to the superior protection of the coating against corrosive media for a long time. As illustrated in Fig. 36 , only one peak appeared in the Bode plot of the Fe3O4–GO/EP nanocomposite, which represented only one time constant for the electrochemical behavior related to the coating. While in the case of other samples, including blank epoxy and the epoxy coating containing GO or Fe3O4 solely, two time-constants were detected associated with charge transferring across the interface besides the poor coating resistance. In contrast, the comparatively long time must elapse for the occurrence of a second time constant for Fe3O4–GO/EP nanocomposite coating. In another study, this group anchored nano-CaCO3 on GO sheets using 3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APTS) as a coupling agent, then dispersed them into an epoxy resin [128]. It was demonstrated that the GO-CaCO3 hybrids lead to a superior corrosion protective coating by its good barrier effects resulting from its uniform dispersion in the matrix, and a hydrophobic characteristic is induced in the coating to prevent the penetration of water into the substrate. As exhibited in Fig. 37 , after the corrosion process, the pure epoxy was degraded, and a large number of holes and cracks formed in the GO/epoxy coating, causing comfortable diffusion of electrolytes into the coating. While just a small number of negligible roughening occurred on the GO-CaCO3/epoxy surface.

Regardless of the advantages of the chemical modification of graphene-based nanosheets, it may cause structural defects and physical properties changes. Non-covalent functionalization would be favorable because of its excellent performance and controllable capability also as it comes with lower adverse side effects on first nanosheets [130]. GO nanosheets can be reduced through water-soluble polymers or small molecules, including poly(sodium 4-styrene sulfonate) [131], sulfonated polyaniline [132], and pyrene butyric acid derivative [133] and form an aqueous solution with well-dispersed GO through π-π interactions. Researchers have also offered some aromatic molecules, which are capable of a couple with GO by π-π stacking, including pyrene derivatives, phthalocyanine derivatives, doxorubicin hydrochloride, and so forth [134]. According to the collected data on GO modified coatings, the above methods are rarely used in the coating. In situ polymerization, as a noncovalent functionalization method, has been widely applied to obtain an exfoliated GO structure in the coating matrices.

Yu et al. successfully synthesized vinyl-modified graphene oxide nanosheets and incorporated them into a polystyrene matrix via an in-situ mini-emulsion polymerization [135]. The chemical bonding of vinyl groups and π-π interactions of GO layers with a polystyrene matrix might contribute to GO layers' exfoliation within the matrix, chemically and physically, respectively. They confirmed that due to the nanocomposite coating's improved barrier properties, resulting from the appropriate dispersion of modified GO, the nanocomposite coating was able to postpone the beginning of corrosion and resist corrosive environments for a long time. Alhumade et al. also successfully developed a well-dispersed network of graphene nanosheets within polyetherimide (PEI) coating via the in situ polymerization process [136]. With regards to Fig. 40 , it can be concluded that the corrosion protection performance of PEI coating was enhanced significantly only by a low content of graphene nanosheets.

Cheng et al. investigated the corrosion resistance of steel by applying Zn-enriched polyurethane coatings along with the effects of graphene nanosheets [137]. The impedance and polarization tests showed that due to graphene's electrical conductivity, the active zinc content improved and had a significant effect on the cathodic protection of the coating. So that the coating with 2 wt. % graphene caused the protection of steel cans for up to 40 days. The corrosion rate of zinc particles has been significantly improved by forming Zn5‏(OH)‏8Cl2 corrosion products on the coating surface. This mechanism is shown in Fig. 41 .

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new category of carbon based nano materials with sizes below 100 nm (mostly range from 3 to 20 nm) [138,139]. This novel material have received increasing attention because of their unique properties including large surface area, non-toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, strong water dispersibility and stable chemical properties [140-144]. CQDs are typically quasi-spherical nanoparticles comprising amorphous to nanocrystalline cores with mainly graphitic or graphene and graphene oxide sheets fused by diamond-like sp3 hybridised carbon insertions (graphene quantum dots (GQDs)) [145-147]. As shown in Fig. 42 , similar to graphene oxide (GO) there are many carboxyl, hydroxyl and carbonyl moieties on the CQD surface, which impart hydrophilic property with strong tendency to dissolve in water and suitable chemically reactive groups for further functionalization. Surface functionalization also modifies their physical properties, like their solubility in aqueous and non-aqueous solvents.

Due to unique and superior properties CQDs widely use in medical imaging, drug detection, photocatalysis, and other biological sciences [148-151]. However, in recent report, Zhu and coworkers reported that CDs which incorporated as a self-healing agent in various polymers successfully improve their anticorrosion properties and open a door for more investigation of these carbon based structure as a anticorrosion agent [152]. The main features of CQDs for application in corrosion resistant coatings are their low toxicity and their capability for production in large-scale via simple low-cost methods which can be competitive with other graphene based materials.

Zhu et al. attributed the inhibiting effect of CQDs to the hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the CQDs adsorbing H2O and trapping oxygen [152]. The O2 molecules adsorbed on the surface of the CQDs are directly reduced to H2O through a fourelectron pathways, thereby suppressing the corrosion process. Ramezanzadeh et al. claimed that the superiority of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQD) in comparison with graphene and graphene oxide nanosheets is their smaller size so that a single atom layered structure GOQD has lateral dimension ranging from 2 to 20 nm. They also pointed out that the lateral dimension of less important particles and what which controls the impermeability of coatings is the size of particles. Therefore, the GOQD with zero dimensions could fill any small and microscopic pores and defects and block the tiny electrolyte pathways in the coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 5-2-Effect of CQDs treatment on the coating protection properties

ce:section.ce:para: Recent research confirmed that heteroatom doping is an effective method for enhancement of the electronic and optical properties of CQDs. The surface of the CQDs contains a large number of reactive groups, such as hydroxyl group, carboxyl group, etc. So, doping the N-containing atom leading to a coordination compound with the outer layer of the iron (d orbital), so that it would be capable to adsorb well on the surface of the carbon steel. Due to the strong electronegativity and electron-donating capability of N atoms, N-CQDs were anticipated as an effective corrosion inhibitor.

Zhixiong Liu et al. prepared N doped carbon dots (NCDs) with aminosalicylic acid (ASA) as precursor under various reaction temperatures and found that these NCDs could effectively inhibit the corrosion of carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution due to its special structure [151]. Cui et al. studied the effect of NCDs concentration on the electrochemistry behaviors of steel in acid solution, and found that the presence of NCDs leaded to a noticeable inhibition effect for steel at 5 mg/L [153]. This team in another research synthesized two NCDs containing amino/hydroxyl functional groups on their surfaces using p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) and ophenylenediamine (o-PD) which showed high inhibitive efficiency for Q235 carbon steel in acid solution [150]. Xiu-Rui Liu et al developed some NCDs from citric acid derivatives and studied their inhibition efficiency on metal in HCl and NaCl solutions. It was found that the corrosion inhibition efficiency was very high at low concentrations of inhibitor. Lv et al. synthesized a new type of N-doped carbon quantum dots using citric acid, diethylenetriamine, and urotropine by microwave method [154]. The synthesized carbon quantum dots have a diameter of about 4–10 nm with an amorphous solid structure containing a large amount of hydrophilic groups such as a hydroxyl group, an amino group, or a carboxyl group on the surface. This novel environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitor has great application prospects which can effectively inhibit the corrosion of carbon steel in 1 M HCl.

Wang et al. synthesized N-CD nanoparticles with diameter of approximately 3–5 nm and added to a waterborne epoxy resin coatings [155]. The fitted coating resistance (Rc) of the prepared coatings up to 70 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution is shown in Fig. 43 . It is significant that the Rc values of pure EP gradually decreases with a high rate due to water absorption. However, by adding 0.5–1.0 wt% N-CDs, the nanocomposite coatings revealed much higher resistance all over the immersion time. Therefore, adding a suitable amount of N-CDs could decrease coating pores and increase barrier properties against the aggressive species in the coating matrix. However, excessive addition of N-CDs (more than 1.0 wt%) leads to spoiling the coating structure and reduction in Rc values. It is noted that increasing in Rc values of N-CDs–EP coatings at the end of the immersion period is because of filling up the defects of the coating.

The hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces and covalent bonding between adjacent NCDs, and between NCDs and polymer chains caused to have high density coating with higer anti-corrosion performance. These nanofillers could fill the pores and defects of the coating, and in other hand may be adsorbed on the metal surface by physical adsorption and chemisorption to form a dense Fe3O4 passivation film leading to reduce corrosion.

Ren et al. prepared a green nitrogen doped carbon dots via solvothermal method and investigated its effect on the anticorrosion properties of a waterborne epoxy (EP) [156]. They found that the functional groups of the NCDs can connect the NCDs and coating to prevent the growth of pores and in other hand, the NCDs by trapping oxygen in the coating, lead to interrupt its connection with substrate. The variation in OCP values of coating/steel in 3.5% NaCl solution as a versus immersion time is presented in Fig. 44 . It can be seen that incorporation of NCDs, lead to significant reduction in the OCP values of composite coatings with different contents of NCDs before 200 h of immersion. Obviously, the OCP of NCDs/EP coatings shifts to a more negative value compared to the blank EP.

The SEM images of the coated with blank EP substrates after 800 h of immersion (Fig. 45 ) shows the accumulation of corrosion products with a rough and porous surface which has been tolerated the severe corrosion. Moreover, with regards to the EDS spectra it can be seen a large amount of O (23.4%), little Cl (2.2%) and Na (0.5%) caused from penetration of corrosion species into the coating (Fig. 45c). It is obvious that in presence of 0.5 wt% of NCDs, just some corrosion region and scratches can be observed on the substrate surface (Fig. 45 d-e) and the content of O and Cl is much less than the substrate coated blank EP, stating the enhancement of the barrier property of the 0.5% NCDs/EP coating (Fig. 45f). In the case of 2.0% NCDs/EP, there is no obvious corrosion on the substrate and the content of O is only 4.6% (Fig. 45 g-i), indicating that the presence of NCDs can interrupt the connection between substrate and oxygen. By increasing the content of the NCDs to 4.0%, the corrosion region becomes relatively rough and a slight content of N (1.6%), Cl (0.2%), O (4.0%) are examined from the metal surface. As a conclusion, the increasing content of NCDs leads to the reduction of the content of O at the metal surface, indicating that the diffusion of oxygen to metal substrate are inhibited remarkably because of the presence of NCDs.

Ye et al. synthesized a new kind of N-doped CQDs through pyrolysis method using natural citric acid derived material, which presented a relatively high inhibition efficiency and low cost (Fig. 46 ) [157]. In another study, they used the functionalized CQDs as an intercalator to disperse graphene nanosheets into epoxy matrix [158]. The structure characterization showed that the graphene achieved high-efficiency dispersion in water and excellent interfacial compatibility in epoxy resin through the modification of functionalized carbon dots.

The electrochemical results showed that the well-dispersed graphene could remarkably enhanced the barrier properties of neat coating and provide a given self-healing properties. Although the addition of graphene could prevent the penetration of corrosive electrolyte, the graphene easily agglomerated in EP coating due to the high specific surface area and interlayer force. Thus, the barrier ability of agglomerate graphene in G0.5%/EP coating was limited. While CQDs modified graphene filled the pores to enhance the density of EP coating, and are capable to well disperse in the EP coating. Moreover, the CQDs adsorbed on the steel surface by the coordination bond and act as a self-healing agent. In order to evaluate the performance of coating against corrosive, the surface roughness of different coatings was investigated (Fig. 47 ). It is obvious that using CQDs modification, resulted in the lowest value of steel surface roughness in the case of CQDs-G0.5 %/EP with the highest coating protection. It is notable that the excessive graphene in CQDs-G1%/EP coating caused to agglomeration and increase the roughness of steel surface.

Pourhashem et al. synthesized GQDs via hydrothermal method using citric acid. A silane coupling agent was used to functionalized the GQDs and modify their interaction with the epoxy matrix [159]. Indeed, silane coupling agent acts as bridging agent between GQDs and polymer matrix and leads to improve the dispersion quality of GQDs in coating. Results show that sufficient amount of functionalized GQDs (f-GQDs) is able to reduce the diffusion of corrosive media through coating and decreases the porosity of epoxy coating. In addition due to the high compatibility of f-GQDs with polymer and lower number of defects, the enlargement rate of pores within the coating reduce significantly in comparison with neat epoxy coating. They claimed that due to the easier preparation of GQDs and f-GQDs and even being more economical than GO and its derivatives, it is reasonable to focus on the potential of GQDs as anti-corrosion pigment in coating systems.

Karimi and Ramezanzadeh successfully synthesized the GOQD by a bottom-up route started from citric acid with 5 nm in width and 0.65 nm in diameter [160]. It was found that due to the much better interfacial interactions, the ultrathin GOQD nanosheets significantly enhanced the mechanical properties of the epoxy coating in comparison with graphene nanosheets. They also found that the GOQD could effectively prevent the crack propagation in the coating. This group in their recent research functionalized GOQD by polyaniline (PANI) and embedded into an epoxy coating [161]. They reported that in the presence of GO-PANI nanomaterial the improvement of the corrosion protection performance of the coating was significant. The obtained results from the accelerated salt spray test which is shown in Fig. 48 revealed that the corrosive electrolyte easily diffused into the neat epoxy coating and caused to delamination of coating. While effected from GOQD-PANI, after the long period of the exposure no corrosion products appeared around the scribe in the nanocomposite coating, indicating the active inhibition role of PANI modified GOQD.

In fact the modification of GOQD with PANI resulted in the lower hydrophilicity of GOQD and its better compatibility of with epoxy matrix. This resulted a highly compact coating with the reduced number of defects and pores, which provide a coating with high barrier performance. In addition, PANI film is able to create well active inhibition. As illustrated in Fig. 48 the presence of GOQD-PANI in epoxy coating leaded to the improvement in the steel interfacial adhesion in wet condition. The GOQD-PANI increased the hydrophobicity of the epoxy coating and decreased the diffusion of water molecules into the coating. According to Fig. 49 , PANI create a passive film based on Fe2O3 and protect the metal surface from corrosion through. Therefore, GOQD-PANI could provide both passive barrier and active inhibition for both un-defected and defected coatings.

Saikia et al. fabricated a bio-based epoxy nanocomposites via in situ polymerization in the presence of polyaniline-CQDs nanohybrid (PC) [162]. The possible structure of the nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 50 , which illustrates the interaction of the different functional groups of the epoxy matrix with the nanomaterial. The graphitic structure-containing oxygenous groups in PC and the nitrogen-containing polymeric backbone of polyaniline nanofiber present strong secondary interactions with the polar functional groups of hyperbranched epoxy matrix. The nanocomposite coatings exhibited great corrosion protection performance saline solution rather than neat epoxy coating, which attributed to the presence of conductive PC part, which blocks the anodic and cathodic sites. In addition, due to the crosslinked structure of the coating, the diffusion of corrosive molecules is retarded by blocking the active sites and developing a torturous path against the corrosive media.

Chen et al. successfully doped Zirconia (ZrO2) on the carbon fiber (CNF) through the electrospinning method and added to the epoxy coating and prepared ZrO2-CNF/epoxy nanocomposite coatings [163]. In order to enhance the dispersion of prepared zirconia doped carbon fiber (ZrO2-CNF) in the epoxy resin the it was modified with γ-(2,3-epoxypropoxy)propytrimethoxysilane (KH560). Electrochemical analysis revealed that the CNF and ZrO2-CNF nanowires could improve the impedance performance of composite coatings effectively. Wang et al. prepared a superhydrophobic polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF)/fluorinated ethylene propylene (FEP)/carbon nanofibers composite coating by a combination of chemical etching and spraying technique [164]. The cross-linked network structure fabricated by CNFs and FEP caused to improved mechanical properties of the coating. The nanocomposite coating illustrated superior hydrophobicity (WCA=164°±1.5°) and excellent corrosion resistant performance. The high corrosion resistance of the coating was attributed to the excellent shielding effect of the superhydrophobic coating leading to the enhanced the diffusion pathway of oxygen.

Yang et al. grown PANI on the carboxylated CNF surface and embedded with Al2O3 particles to the polyphenylene sulfide (PPS) and ethylene tetrafluoroethylene (ETFE) (PPS/ETFE) to developed a superhydrophobic coating [165]. They found that the in existence of the electroactive PANI/CNF nanofillers, the superhydrophobicity and durability of the coatings improved significantly. The electrochemical analysis revealed an excellent corrosion protection performance (99.993%) after 40 d immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. They introduced an anti-corrosion mechanism which illustrated in Fig. 51 . According to the mechanism, corrosive agents is prevented to reach the underlying substrate by barrier effect of PANI/CNF in primary stage of failure (Fig. 51a). In addition the nanocomposite coating reveal a more stable and durable superhydrophobicity and stable barrierity In comparison with PANI coatings. Moreover, the nanostructured PANI/CNF nanocomposite improves the adhesion and mechanical properties of the coating through cross-linking. Another mechanism which is govern here would be attributed to the passivation effect of PANI, protecting the substrate from acceleration of pitting corrosion (Fig. 51b). The interface between Al substrate and PANI/CNF may also yield an electric field, which would retard electron transfer from Al to the oxidant.

The corrosion resistance and efficiency values are dependent on the substrate, coating matrix, coating conditions, corrosive environment, nanoparticle types, and so on. Consequently, it is difficult to compare the coatings' corrosion resistance and evaluate the effects of various nanoparticles on their corrosion resistance. However, it would be an appropriate way to calculate each conductive nano-coatings corrosion efficiency based on its neat form. Therefore, the corrosion protection efficiency of the modified nano-coatings concerning the neat coatings could be estimated using the Eqs. (1) and (2) [166]:

Eq. 1 (Icorr ) Blank (Icorr ) nano represents the corrosion currents of the neat coating and nano coating, respectively. In Eq. 2, RBlank and R
nano are hinted to the corrosion resistance of the neat coating and nano coating, respectively which can be obtained by potentiodynamic polarization testing as pore resistance or polarization resistance (obtained from Stern-Gerry equation [167]) or by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy as coating resistance or polarization resistance (sum of coating and charge transfer resistances [168]).

Table 1 represents some of the recent researches on the anti-corrosion properties of organic coatings containing carbon nanostructures, including graphene-based sheets (G and GO) and its derivatives, CNT and CB. Nanoparticles mainly act as physical barriers between the metal surface and the corrosive species like oxygen, water, and corrosive ions. They reduce the corrosion of the metal and show strong protective properties. Finely dispersed particles can fill the micropores and defects present within the coating structure, resulting in a reduced total free volume and improving the packing density of the composite. Hence, incorporating nanoparticles into the coatings causes an increase in the integrity and superior protection of coatings during long-time exposure in corrosive media. Conductive nanoparticles can also provide a cathodic reaction across the metal-electrolyte interface, which leads to corrosion inhibition. Also, various carbon-based compounds' modifications improve the barrier action of nanocomposite and induce chemical interactions of the nanoparticles with the functional groups of the resin matrix and/or metal substrate. This may deduce higher coating strength due to the crosslink density/toughness reinforcement and consequently enhance the polymer structure mechanical properties and create more excellent bonding with substrate leading to improvement of the coating adhesion to the metal surface. Also, it was previously reported that the nanoparticles' physical and chemical grafting with the organic/inorganic corrosion inhibitor species could add the active inhibition/passivation role to the coating system besides the barrier performance improvement.

The given data in Table 1 shows that the optimum nanoparticle content loaded in the coatings has been reported lower than 1 wt. % in different cases. It was observed that the use of nanoparticle more than optimum content in the coating causes the reduction in the corrosion protection degree of the nanocoatings, which mostly has related to the agglomeration of nanoparticles and reduction in adhesion properties of the coating.

It can be seen that the modified nanoparticles lead to better protection compared to the unmodified nanoparticles, especially in long immersion time. Generally, nanoparticles' modification considerably leads to weak intermolecular interactions and prevents fillers from agglomeration to improve its dispersion state. Furthermore, it can boost the interfacial interaction of nanoparticles with the polymer matrix by covalent or non-covalent bonding. Thus, nanoparticles' modification would be a simple method to develop nanocomposite coatings with effectively enhanced protection properties.

However, CNTs and CB nanoparticles exhibited desirable protection performance. It can be seen that the well-dispersed graphene nanosheets showed better performance with a lower percentage in comparison with CNT and CB. This can be attributed to the flaky geometry, high aspect ratio, and superior surface area of the graphene nanosheets compared with other fillers, which cause its great potential to be a favorable barrier to penetration of corrosive species.
